notes: Formulas are evaluated at runtime with actual headcount. pack_rounding=true means the engine rounds up to the nearest full pack_size. severity_thresholds are coverage ratios: qty_in_cart / qty_required.

rules.notes: Kids use 2 plates at parties — food plate and cake plate. Adults also do 2 rounds at sit-down events. 20-piece minimum floors small gatherings.
Kids get juice + cold drink separately. Adults get 2 drink rounds minimum. Cricket match bumps this to 3 but the base formula is shared.
Kids are significantly messier than adults. 5 napkins per child is the observed average at Indian birthday parties. Napkins are cheap so over-ordering is preferred.
Adults use 3 napkins at a seated meal or party. Potluck setting needs at least 3 for multi-course finger food.
3 balloons per child is the Indian birthday party visual minimum — some pop, some are given away. Most packs are 50-count so a single pack covers up to ~16 kids.
Adult and baby shower events need fewer balloons — 2 per guest is sufficient for visual decor without covering the floor.
Birthday candles are binary — you need exactly 1 set (number candle or age-appropriate pack). The severity is binary: present or absent. Missing candles is always a critical flag.
Diwali lighting requires 5+ diyas or candles per household member as the central ritual act. Grihapravesh needs minimum 10 for all room blessings.
One tablecloth per event is sufficient for the main food table. Flagged as low severity only — it protects the table and reads well in photos but is not functional-critical.
2 streamer rolls minimum for a decorated space — one for the entrance arch, one for the backdrop or ceiling. More than 4 is decorative excess, not a meaningful addition.
One return gift per attending guest is the Indian social expectation. Undercounting is a social embarrassment — running out mid-party means some kids go home empty-handed. Round up aggressively.
One banner is the visual focal point for party photos. Missing banner is flagged low — it is strongly recommended but not functionally critical.
2 bottles per trekker: one for water, one for electrolyte drink or reserve. Single-bottle setup risks dehydration on long ascents. Low minimum of 2 for solo trekkers.
3 energy bars per trekker per day of a standard day-trek. Multi-day treks need this multiplied by trip days — formula assumes 1-day default. Under 40% is a safety issue on strenuous trails.
First aid kit is binary — present or absent. Absence on any trek is always critical. One kit per group is sufficient. If absent, block the trip completion message in the demo.
1 bag of chips per person as a baseline snack. For cricket parties, the formula reflects that chips are the primary food — undercounting is noticeable. 2-pack minimum for any gathering.
Chocolates are for distribution and consumption — 2 per guest accounts for 1 to eat and 1 for the gift plate or prasad. Diwali gifting demands at least 2 per visitor.
Serving spoons — estimate 1 dish per 5 people at a potluck. These are serving utensils, not personal spoons. Minimum 3 for any multi-dish setup.
1 biscuit pack per person as a snack supplement. Popular as a light food backup at potlucks. Minimum 2 packs regardless of headcount.
1 juice carton/bottle per guest. Kids birthday is the strongest signal — juice is the primary cold drink for children. Minimum 3 for any gathering to avoid running out before the cake cutting.
1 soda/cold drink per guest for adult events. Cricket watching drives higher consumption — the formula is a conservative baseline. Minimum 3 for any gathering.
2 scented candles for ambience — one near the entrance, one on the main table. Grihapravesh and Diwali have atmospheric scent as a cultural element. Severity is low — it is a nice-to-have.
1 torch per 2 trekkers for overnight or early-morning treks on unlit trails. At least 1 per group regardless of size. Under 50% coverage on a multi-day trek is a safety issue.
